claims.text: Cross-domain reinforcement learning (CDRL) aims to improve data efficiency of RL by leveraging data samples collected from a source domain to facilitate learning in a similar target domain.
claims.source_quote: Cross-domain reinforcement learning (CDRL) is meant to improve the data efficiency of RL by leveraging the data samples collected from a source domain to facilitate the learning in a similar target domain.

claims.text: Cross-domain transfer in RL has two fundamental challenges: distinct state/action spaces between source and target domains, and difficulty in identifying transferability of a source-domain model a priori.
claims.source_quote: cross-domain transfer in RL is known to have two fundamental and intertwined challenges: (i) The source and target domains can have distinct state space or action space, and this makes direct transfer infeasible and thereby requires more sophisticated inter-domain mappings; (ii) The transferability of a source-domain model in RL is not easily identifiable a priori, and hence CDRL can be prone to negative effect during transfer.

claims.text: Distinct state or action spaces between source and target domains make direct transfer infeasible.
claims.source_quote: The source and target domains can have distinct state space or action space, and this makes direct transfer infeasible and thereby requires more sophisticated inter-domain mappings

claims.text: CDRL can be prone to negative transfer effects when the transferability of a source-domain model is not identifiable a priori.
claims.source_quote: The transferability of a source-domain model in RL is not easily identifiable a priori, and hence CDRL can be prone to negative effect during transfer.

claims.text: The authors introduce the notion of cross-domain Bellman consistency as a measure of transferability of a source-domain model.
claims.source_quote: we first introduce the notion of cross-domain Bellman consistency as a way to measure transferability of a source-domain model.

claims.text: QAvatar combines Q functions from both the source and target domains using an adaptive hyperparameter-free weight function.
claims.source_quote: we propose QAvatar, which combines the Q functions from both the source and target domains with an adaptive hyperparameter-free weight function.

claims.text: QAvatar achieves reliable transfer by effectively leveraging a source-domain Q function for knowledge transfer to the target domain.
claims.source_quote: we characterize the convergence behavior of QAvatar and show that QAvatar achieves reliable transfer in the sense that it effectively leverages a source-domain Q function for knowledge transfer to the target domain.

claims.text: QAvatar achieves favorable transferability across various RL benchmark tasks including locomotion and robot arm manipulation.
claims.source_quote: Through experiments, we demonstrate that QAvatar achieves favorable transferability across various RL benchmark tasks, including locomotion and robot arm manipulation.

claims.text: The convergence behavior of QAvatar is characterized theoretically.
claims.source_quote: we characterize the convergence behavior of QAvatar and show that QAvatar achieves reliable transfer

claims.text: The code for QAvatar is publicly available at https://rl-bandits-lab.github.io/Cross-Domain-RL/.
